sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 123, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Agent at rows 18-23, can't move further DOWN. Blocks move DOWN when agent moves DOWN. Need to test if blocks move UP when agent moves UP.
- Plan: Move agent UP and observe if blocks move UP or stay fixed.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 122 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key findings:
- UP,UP: gameplay_changed=False (agent already at top, can't move further UP)
- Agent at rows 12-17, blocks at rows 25-28 (red), 31-34 (blue), 37-40 (green)
- UPx3: gameplay_changed=False (agent still at top)
So the agent is at the top of its range (rows 12-17). The agent can't move further UP.
The agent's range is rows 12-23 (approximately). The agent can move UP to rows 12-17 and DOWN to rows 18-23.
Wait, but earlier the agent was at rows 36-41 (initial position). How did the agent get from rows 36-41 to rows 12-17?
Let me re-examine. The initial state had the agent at rows 36-41. I moved the agent UP many times. But the agent's range is only rows 12-23.
This suggests that the agent's position is not what I think it is. Maybe the agent's position is relative to the panel, not the grid.
Or maybe the panel scrolls, and the agent's position within the panel is fixed.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the agent is at rows 12-17, which is at the top of the panel (rows 12-41).
The initial state had the agent at rows 36-41, which is at the bottom of the panel.
So the agent can move from the top of the panel (rows 12-17) to the bottom of the panel (rows 36-41).
But the agent's current range is only rows 12-23. This suggests that the agent hit a boundary when moving DOWN.
Wait, let me re-examine. Earlier, I moved the agent DOWN from rows 12-17 to rows 18-23 (2 DOWN actions). Then I tried to move DOWN more, but the agent couldn't move further.
But the initial state had the agent at rows 36-41. So the agent should be able to move from rows 12-17 to rows 36-41.
Unless... the agent's range changed. Maybe the agent's range depends on the trail length or some other factor.
Actually, looking at the current segmentation, the horizontal trail is at rows 12-13, cols 17-46. This is a 2-row trail.
When the agent is at rows 12-17, the trail is at rows 12-13, which is above the agent.
This is strange. The trail should be at the agent's row, not above it.
Let me look at the image again. The agent is at the top-left of the panel, and the trail extends to the right.
The trail is at the agent's row, which is rows 12-17.
But the segmentation shows the trail at rows 12-13. This suggests that the trail is only 2 rows tall, not 6 rows tall.
Actually, looking at the image, the trail is a thin horizontal line, not a thick rectangle. So the trail is 2 rows tall (rows 12-13).
The agent is at rows 12-17, which is 6 rows tall. The trail is at rows 12-13, which is the top 2 rows of the agent.
So the trail is at the top of the agent, not the center.
OK, I think I understand the game better now. The agent is a 6-row tall rectangle. The trail extends from the top of the agent to the right.
When the agent moves DOWN, the trail moves DOWN with the agent. The trail is always at the top 2 rows of the agent.
The blocks are at fixed positions within the panel. When the agent moves, the blocks stay in the same position.
But the segmentation shows the blocks at different rows. This is because the blocks move when the agent moves.
Wait, but I just observed that when the agent moves UP, the blocks don't move (gameplay_changed=False).
And when the agent moves DOWN, the blocks move DOWN (gameplay_changed=True).
This suggests that the blocks only move when the agent moves DOWN, not when the agent moves UP.
This is asymmetric behavior. Let me think about what this means.
Maybe the blocks are "falling" when the agent moves DOWN. The agent pushes the blocks DOWN.
But when the agent moves UP, the blocks don't move UP. They stay in place.
This would explain why the blocks are at rows 25-28 (red), 31-34 (blue), 37-40 (green) when the agent is at rows 12-17.
Initially, the blocks were at rows 19-22 (red), 25-28 (blue), 31-34 (green) when the agent was at rows 36-41.
When the agent moved UP from rows 36-41 to rows 12-17, the blocks stayed at rows 19-22, 25-28, 31-34.
But then when the agent moved DOWN from rows 12-17 to rows 18-23, the blocks moved DOWN to rows 25-28, 31-34, 37-40.
So the blocks moved DOWN by 6 rows when the agent moved DOWN by 6 rows.
But when the agent moved UP, the blocks didn't move UP.
This confirms that the blocks only move DOWN when the agent moves DOWN.
So the blocks are "falling" when the agent moves DOWN. The agent pushes the blocks DOWN.
But when the agent moves UP, the blocks don't move UP. They stay in place.
This means the blocks can only move DOWN, not UP.
So if I move the agent DOWN, the blocks move DOWN. If I then move the agent UP, the blocks stay at their new position.
This is a one-way mechanism. The blocks can only move DOWN.
So the goal might be to move the blocks to a specific position by moving the agent DOWN.
The palette shows: red, green, blue.
The panel shows: red, blue, green.
Maybe the goal is to rearrange the blocks to match the palette's order.
But the blocks move DOWN together, maintaining their relative order. So I can't rearrange them by moving the agent DOWN.
Unless... the blocks can be rearranged by some other action.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to have the trail overlap the blocks in a specific order.
The trail is at the top 2 rows of the agent. When the agent is at rows 12-17, the trail is at rows 12-13.
The red block is at rows 25-28. The trail is above the red block.
If I move the agent DOWN, the trail moves DOWN. When the agent is at rows 23-28, the trail is at rows 23-24.
The red block is at rows 25-28. The trail would be just above the red block.
If I move the agent DOWN more, the trail would overlap the red block.
But the agent can only move DOWN to rows 18-23 (from the current position of rows 12-17).
Wait, the agent is currently at rows 12-17. If I move the agent DOWN, the agent moves to rows 18-23.
The trail is at rows 18-19. The red block is at rows 25-28. The trail is still above the red block.
If I move the agent DOWN more, the agent would move to rows 24-29. But earlier, the agent couldn't move further DOWN from rows 18-23.
Wait, but the blocks moved DOWN when the agent moved DOWN. So the agent's range might have changed.
Let me try moving the agent DOWN again and see if it can move further.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Agent at rows 12-17 (top), blocks at rows 25-28 (red), 31-34 (blue), 37-40 (green). Blocks move DOWN when agent moves DOWN, but not UP when agent moves UP. Agent's range seems limited.
Plan: Try moving agent DOWN to see if range expanded after blocks moved. Also try ACTION7 to see if it enables new movement.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving agent DOWN from current position (rows 12-17)
# Earlier agent moved from 12-17 to 18-23 with 2 DOWN actions
# Let's try more DOWN actions
result = action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
print(f"DOWNx6: gameplay_changed={result.get('gameplay_changed')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

# Check agent and block positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 5 and n['pixels'] < 100:
        b = n['boundary']
        r_min, r_max = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c_min, c_max = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        if r_min < 55:
            print(f"  {n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={r_min}-{r_max} cols={c_min}-{c_max}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  W pixels=12 rows=19-22 cols=12-15

  G pixels=8 rows=28-31 cols=13-14
  b pixels=16 rows=31-34 cols=42-45
  G pixels=8 rows=34-37 cols=13-14
  N pixels=16 rows=37-40 cols=42-45
  g pixels=31 rows=53-53 cols=0-30
  G pixels=33 rows=53-53 cols=31-63
